name: Conall Cernach
description: Conall Cernach, meaning "Conall the Victorious", is a prominent hero in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology. He was a renowned warrior of the Red Branch, known for his unwavering loyalty, fierce bravery, and exceptional combat skills. Often depicted as a physically imposing figure with a red beard, Conall was a protector of Ulster and a close companion of Cú Chulainn. He avenged the death of Cú Chulainn's foster-brother, Froech, and played a crucial role in many battles, demonstrating his exceptional prowess and dedication to his people. Conall was bound by a strict code of honor and was known for his boastfulness and competitive nature, often engaging in contests of strength and skill with other heroes. His most famous deed is undoubtedly his relentless pursuit and eventual slaying of the killer of Cú Chulainn, Lugaid mac Con Roi, solidifying his reputation as a fierce avenger and a loyal friend. His character embodies the warrior ethos of early Irish society, emphasizing courage, loyalty, and martial excellence. He is a central figure in many tales, showcasing his importance to the narrative and the values of the Ulster Cycle. Conall's unwavering commitment to his comrades and his willingness to face any challenge make him a compelling and enduring figure in Irish legend. He represents the ideals of a heroic warrior, demonstrating strength, loyalty, and a relentless pursuit of justice.
subtitle: Victorious Warrior of the Red Branch
achievements: Becoming a champion of the Red Branch

keyMyths.title: The Death of Froech
keyMyths.description: Froech is murdered while bathing, prompting Cú Chulainn to seek revenge. Conall Cernach aids him, showcasing their bond and Conall's loyalty.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Lugaid
keyMyths.description: Conall Cernach vows to avenge Cú Chulainn's death and relentlessly pursues Lugaid mac Con Roi. After a long chase, Conall defeats and kills Lugaid, fulfilling his oath.

keyMyths.title: Conall's Oath
keyMyths.description: Conall Cernach makes an oath to never go a night without a Connachtman to kill, rob, or hold prisoner. This reveals his fierce warrior nature and commitment to Ulster.

sources.author: Thomas Kinsella
sources.description: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, featuring Conall Cernach's involvement in the conflict.
